data.title: IMF’s Georgieva warns central banks against rushing to hike rates amid recession fears
data.description:
International Monetary Fund Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva cautioned central banks against moving too quickly to raise interest rates, warning that premature action “could be very dangerous because it would suffocate growth.”

The warning comes as the IMF downgraded its global growth forecast and raised concerns that a prolonged war in the Middle East could push the world economy toward recession.

In a Bloomberg TV interview, Georgieva noted that while short-term inflation expectations have increased, the rise has been modest—with the IMF raising its inflation projection from 3.8% to 4.2%.

Crucially, she emphasized that long-term inflation expectations remain “well anchored,” suggesting central banks with strong credibility can afford to take a “wait and see attitude” rather than acting preemptively.

“My worry is because of '22, now central banks may say, let’s move faster, and that could be very dangerous because it would suffocate growth,” she said.

Georgieva addressed what she views as excessive market optimism, particularly in the U.S., noting a disconnect between U.S. market exuberance and conditions elsewhere. She attributed the optimism to the high concentration of financial assets in America and the country’s position as an oil exporter rather than importer.

“I can tell you that’s not the story for the rest of the world. In the rest of the world, already, there is a lot of pain,” she said, adding that supply chain disruptions and infrastructure destruction have impacts already “baked in.”

The IMF chief praised the United Kingdom’s “mature approach to policy” as a model for fiscal responsibility, highlighting targeted and temporary spending within fiscal boundaries.

She urged fiscal authorities globally to build buffers during good times and avoid “indiscriminate spending,” noting that pro-growth policies represent the best defense against future shocks.

On central bank independence, Georgieva was unequivocal, stating that “independent central banks are an economic asset” backed by decades of evidence. She pointed out that emerging market central banks have actually outperformed their advanced economy peers by maintaining data-dependent policy making and institutional independence.

Looking ahead, Georgieva described the current environment as a “more shock-prone world” where economic shocks will continue to arrive in layers.

While a durable ceasefire in the Middle East could accelerate recovery, she stressed that “we all need to learn to operate in an environment of high-end and permanent uncertainty,” calling for agility and adaptability from policymakers worldwide.

More on interest rates

  • Gold sinks as Trump's Iran speech seen raising likelihood of higher interest rates
  • The Fed will cut interest rates ‘a whole bunch of times…substantially more than two’ – Greenlight Capital’s David Einhorn
  • The Fed is more likely to raise interest rates than to cut – WisdomTree’s Jeremy Siegel
  • Chicago Fed’s Goolsbee: Inflation shocks from the energy markets crisis will delay interest rates in 2026

data.title: Kalshi ramps up commodities expansion amid dynamic geopolitical environment
data.description:
With the Iran war and other geopolitical events roiling commodity markets, prediction markets platform Kalshi dramatically expanded the types of commodities contracts it's offering, the company said on Wednesday.

In addition to the trading it already offers for WTI and Brent oil, gold, and silver, Kalshi (KALSHI) is adding natural gas, coffee, copper, sugar, corn, soybeans, wheat, nickel, diesel, and lithium. The commodities event contracts will be grouped in a dedicated hub on the platform.

Individuals and institutions will be able to trade on the future direction of those key commodities 24/7, it added.

data.title: South Korea's best-rated stocks: From financials to semiconductors, ranked by Quant scores
data.description:
A Yardeni Research chart tracking South Korea's MSCI forward earnings per share tells that from the early 1990s through the mid-2010s, South Korean forward EPS moved in a slow, decades-long upward arc.

But starting in 2025, the line went nearly vertical.

Against this backdrop of historic earnings momentum, the following list ranks the primary South Korean stocks available to U.S. investors by Seeking Alpha Quant Rating, spanning financials, industrials, semiconductors, telecommunications, and utilities, with Shinhan Financial Group (SHG), POSCO Holdings (PKX), and KB Financial Group (KB) leading the pack with Strong Buy ratings above 4.78.

Further down the list, Woori Financial Group Inc. (WF) and SK Telecom Co., Ltd. (SKM) both carry Hold ratings at 3.47. The remaining stocks on the list include a diverse mix of companies, from Magnachip Semiconductor Corporation (MX) in the chip sector to Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEP) in utilities, all maintaining Hold ratings with scores ranging from 3.38 down to 2.89.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings system grades stocks based on their relative performance across critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered bullish and any rating of 2.5 or below considered bearish.

Here is the list:

  1. Shinhan Financial Group Co., Ltd. (SHG), Quant Rating: 4.86

  2. POSCO Holdings Inc. (PKX), Quant Rating: 4.83

  3. KB Financial Group Inc. (KB), Quant Rating: 4.78

  4. Woori Financial Group Inc. (WF), Quant Rating: 3.47

  5. SK Telecom Co., Ltd. (SKM), Quant Rating: 3.47

  6. Magnachip Semiconductor Corporation (MX), Quant Rating: 3.38

  7. KT Corporation (KT), Quant Rating: 3.27

  8. Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEP), Quant Rating: 3.09

  9. DoubleDown Interactive Co., Ltd. (DDI), Quant Rating: 3.03

  10. LG Display Co., Ltd. (LPL), Quant Rating: 2.96

  11. OSR Holdings, Inc. (OSRH), Quant Rating: 2.89

data.title: Energy Recovery cut at Northcoast as Iran war raises odds of additional project delays
data.description:
Energy Recovery (ERII) down 5.2% in Wednesday's trading as Northcoast Research downgraded the maker of energy efficiency devices in the water industry to Neutral from Buy, saying long-term fundamentals remain intact but anticipating elevated tactical risk on likely Middle East project delays.

When Energy Recovery (ERII) posted Q4 earnings and guided FY 2026 revenues below expectations due to project delays, the Iran war had not yet begun, and the macro outlook forthe critical Middle East region has since taken a decided turn for the worse, with sizeable declines in GDP likely ahead for many major desalinating nations and a growing risk that the war will prove a lasting setback for confidence in the Middle East economy, Northcoast's Ryan Connors wrote.

Water may be non-discretionary, but the timing of projects—some of which are predicated on future demand growth put at risk by the Iran conflict—can be altered significantly, so the analyst lowered his 2026 and 2027 earnings estimates for Energy Recovery (ERII) to reflect the rising probability of additional project delays amid a major macroeconomic headwind that weighs on fiscal budgets and infrastructure spending.

While still off from pre-Q4 print, Energy Recovery (ERII) shares have bounced nicely off recent lows, and the risk/reward looks balanced heading into the company's Q1 earnings and guidance update; with shares trading at ~20x Connors' 2027 EPS estimate, roughly in line with the waterinfrastructure peer group average, the analyst dropped his rating to Neutral.

data.title: Sazerac offered to buy out Brown-Forman for $15B - WSJ
data.description:
Sazerac offered to buy Brown-Forman (BF.A) (BF.B) for about $15B, or $32 per share, sources told The Wall Street Journal. Shares of the Jack Daniel's maker rose sharply last week after an initial report on privately-owned Sazerac's interest in a potential acquisition. Last month, Pernod Ricard SA (PDRDF) was said to be weighing a potential purchase of Brown-Forman (BF.A) (BF.B).

A merger between the two spirits powerhouses would combine Sazerac labels such as Buffalo Trace, Blanton’s, Weller, Eagle Rare, Fireball, Southern Comfort, and RTD brand BuzzBallz, with Brown-Forman (BF.A) (BF.B) brands including Jack Daniel’s, Woodford Reserve, Old Forester, Herradura tequila, and el Jimador tequila.

Shares of Brown-Forman (BF.A) (BF.B) were swapping hands at $29.25 in late Wednesday afternoon trading.

data.title: Hemisphere Energy Corporation GAAP EPS of $0.05, revenue of $18.99M
data.description:
• Hemisphere Energy Corporation press release (HME:CA): Q4 AFFO of $0.10
  • GAAP EPS of $0.05.
  • Revenue of $18.99M.
  • Increased annual production by 6% to a record 3,645 boe/d (99% heavy oil).
  • Generated annual revenue of $93.9 million.
  • Achieved $42.9 million in annual adjusted funds flow from operations ("AFF")(1).
  • Invested $16.3 million in capital expenditures(1) focused on Hemisphere's long life, low decline assets.
  • Generated $26.6 million of annual free funds flow ("FFF")(1), an 11% increase over annual 2024 FFF.
  • Achieved total operating and transportation costs of $15.14/boe.
  • Distributed $9.6 million in quarterly base dividends to shareholders during the year.
  • Distributed $5.8 million in special dividends to shareholders during the year.
  • Returned $6.5 million to shareholders during the year with the purchase and cancellation of 3.5 million shares under the Company's normal course issuer bid ("NCIB"), at an average price of $1.88/share.
  • Exited 2025 with a positive working capital(1) position of $8.6 million compared to $6.4 million at year-end 2024.

data.title: Pnc expects 2026 net interest income up 14.5% following FirstBank deal and 11% average loan growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The PNC Financial Services Group (PNC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We completed the acquisition of FirstBank early in the quarter, and we're well on our way to a mid-June conversion,” said “Organic loan growth hit a 3-year high, net interest margin expanded meaningfully, and we had 13% year-over-year fee income growth” (Chairman & CEO William Demchak).
  • “We are not seeing anything that suggests these issues are broadly impacting our customers or our credit quality in the near term,” Demchak said, adding on nondepository financial institution exposure: “we don't see any loss content in this book and certainly don't see any exposure to a systemic event.” (Chairman & CEO Demchak).
  • “As a result, our overall balance sheet growth includes the impact of the acquisition, which represented $15 billion in loans and $22 billion in deposits,” and “during the quarter, we returned $1.4 billion of capital to shareholders,” including “approximately $700 million each” in dividends and buybacks (Executive VP & CFO Robert Reilly).

Outlook

  • “Our outlook for the second quarter of 2026 compared to the first quarter of 2026 is as follows: we expect average loans to be up 2% to 3%, net interest income to be up approximately 3%, fee income to be up 2.5%, other noninterest income to be in the range of $150 million to $200 million,” and “we expect total revenue to be up approximately 3.5%” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • “Our outlook for the full year 2026 compared to 2025 results is as follows: we expect full year average loan growth to be up approximately 11%,” “full year net interest income to be up approximately 14.5%,” “noninterest income to be up approximately 6%,” and “total revenue to be up approximately 11%” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Change vs. prior-quarter framing: in Q4 2025, Reilly said, “We expect the Fed to cut rates 2x in 2026,” while in Q1 2026, Reilly updated, “We do not expect the Federal Reserve to cut rates during 2026” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).

Financial Results

  • “For the linked quarter, loans of $351 billion grew by $23 billion or 7%,” “deposit balances were up $19 billion or 4% and averaged $458 billion,” and “we remain well capitalized with an estimated CET1 ratio of 10.1%, down 50 basis points from year-end 2025” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • “Total revenue was $6.2 billion,” “net interest income of $4 billion increased $230 million or 6%,” and “our net interest margin was 2.95%, an increase of 11 basis points” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Profitability and items impacting comparability: “our first quarter net income was $1.8 billion or $4.13 per common share and $4.32 when adjusted for integration costs,” while “noninterest expense of $3.8 billion increased $165 million or 5%, of which $97 million was integration expense” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Credit and private credit/NDFI detail: “nonperforming loans… represented 0.62% of total loans,” “our allowance for credit losses totaled $5.5 billion or 1.52% of total loans,” and Reilly said the business credit intermediaries portion includes “approximately $7 billion” mostly CLOs, concluding, “expect zero losses going forward” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).

Q&A

  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: asked about growing core deposits in a higher-for-longer rate environment; Chairman & CEO Demchak responded, “our focus has been on growing retail clients,” adding, “We’ve opened [8] so far this year,” and “Our digital acquisition has been really strong.”
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: pressed on conservatism in loan-growth outlook; Executive VP & CFO Reilly said, “we do have some paydowns that are coming,” and Chairman & CEO Demchak added, “We can't predict it, but we banked some in the first quarter.”
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked why higher loan-growth outlook doesn’t translate proportionally to NII; Reilly said, “it's loan mix,” with “a much higher volume of higher credit quality deals… carry relatively lower spreads,” while reiterating, “We still expect to go above 3% in the second half.”
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: asked on capital markets pipelines and M&A; Reilly said, “their pipelines are strong,” and “for the full year, still up double digits” (re Harris Williams) (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous: asked on FirstBank conversion timing and expense cadence; Reilly said, “We are still planning to convert mid-June,” with “nonrecurring merger and integration costs… approximately $325 million,” including “approximately $150 million in the second quarter” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: challenged private credit loss concerns; Demchak responded, “It's not even on the curve,” and “this isn't even on the page of what we're looking at.”
  • Matthew O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: asked about rate positioning and MSR hedges; Demchak said, “We are basically economic value of capital flat,” and on MSR, “we got chopped up… realized vol was way higher than implied.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment: slightly negative to pressing, with repeated questions on deposit competition, loan-growth durability, private credit/NDFI exposure, and Basel capital usage.
  • Management sentiment: slightly positive in prepared remarks (“we're off to a really strong start this year”) and more emphatic in Q&A on NDFI (“expect zero losses going forward”) while acknowledging volatility impacts (“we got chopped up”).
  • Versus last quarter: tone stayed constructive, but Q1 2026 included more direct rebuttal of market worries around private credit and a clearer rates stance (“We do not expect the Federal Reserve to cut rates during 2026”) versus Q4 2025’s projected cuts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategy continuity with added emphasis: both quarters highlighted FirstBank integration and branch/technology investment, while Q1 2026 put more weight on defending NDFI/private credit exposure and detailing composition (trade receivable securitizations vs. CLOs).
  • Guidance language shifted on rates: Q4 2025 guided “cut rates 2x in 2026,” while Q1 2026 stated no cuts expected; management also reiterated buyback pace expectations (“range of $600 million to $700 million going forward”).
  • Key operating momentum discussed in Q1 2026 included “net interest margin… 2.95%” and CRE “reached an inflection point,” reinforcing prior-quarter commentary that CRE had “largely stabilized” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).

Risks and Concerns

  • “Many market concerns out there from energy prices to AI to private credit,” but management said, “we are not seeing anything” broadly affecting customers near term (Chairman & CEO Demchak).
  • Deposit competition was acknowledged: Demchak noted “people are paying up to maintain balances,” while asserting PNC “don't have to go and lead with our faces here on price.”
  • Earnings sensitivity to volatility showed up in mortgage/MSR: “Mortgage revenue decreased… largely attributable to… heightened rate volatility,” and Demchak said MSR hedging was hurt as “realized vol was way higher than implied” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly; Chairman & CEO Demchak).

Final Takeaway

PNC’s management characterized Q1 2026 as a “really strong start,” anchored by FirstBank’s addition and legacy C&I growth, alongside an updated rates view that assumes no Fed cuts in 2026. Reilly’s outlook called for Q2 revenue up about 3.5% and full-year 2026 net interest income up about 14.5%, while management repeatedly pushed back on investor concerns about private credit exposure, emphasizing the investment-grade and collateralized nature of the NDFI book and stating they “expect zero losses going forward.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BP raised to Buy at UBS as new CEO takes over 'at critical turning pont'
data.description:
BP (BP) is little changed in Wednesday's trading as UBS upgraded shares to Buy from Neutral, as CEO Meg O'Neill takes over at "a critical turning point for the company," with work to do to regain investor confidence and reduce the stock's 50%-plus underperformance compared to peers since 2018.

UBS analyst Joshua Stone identified four potential areas of focus ahead of an expected strategy update later in the year, including right-sizing the company's cost base, noting BP (BP) has the highest cost intensity of peers, with total operating expenses up ~$10B since 2019.

Stone's analysis suggests $3B-$6B in potential savings from current levels, which represents a 20% increase to pre-tax earnings at the midpoint.

BP (BP) also has the highest leverage ratio in its sector at 47%, and higher prices help to speed up deleveraging, with Stone saying he now sees the ratio falling to 27% by 2028 in a base case scenario; the analyst thinks capex can be held at $13B-$14B until 2027, but may need to increase to ~$15B from 2028 to fund new projects.

data.title: Bessent sees oil prices falling to around $3/gallon this summer
data.description:
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he sees oil prices falling to around $3 per gallon over the summer.

“I’m optimistic that sometime between June 20 and September 20, we can have $3 gas again,” Bessent told reporters at the White House on Wednesday, according to Bloomberg.

Bessent added that officials from several Middle Eastern countries have said that once the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, they can begin pumping oil within one week. 

Bloomberg noted that the average price of a gallon of gas this week was $4.108, citing the American Automobile Association.

Bessent also said the government would be monitoring gas stations to make sure their prices are in line with the price of oil.

“We are going to be watching the gas stations, because they raised prices very quickly,” Bessent said, adding, “We hope they’ll bring them down just as quickly as crude oil prices have come down, which they’ve come down substantially, just in the past 10 days.”
